methods.method_name: MicrogAMBI Index Calculation and Taxa Assignment
methods.description: This method describes the process for identifying microbial taxa, assigning them to ecological groups (EGs) based on surveyed literature and their association with pollution inputs, and calculating the microgAMBI index from the relative abundance ratio of sensitive/indifferent (EGI) and tolerant/opportunistic (EGIII) taxa to classify ecological status. It also includes the process for expanding the taxa list and defining criteria for EG assignment.

steps.description: Microbial taxa were identified via the taxonomic classification of genomic sequences
steps.searchable_summary: Perform taxonomic classification of genomic sequences to identify microbial taxa

steps.description: assigned to EGs based on surveyed literature, as described in the initial paper [5].
steps.searchable_summary: Assign identified microbial taxa to Ecological Groups (EGs) based on surveyed literature

steps.description: Following the principle of AMBI, which was simplified to two single responses, microbes not associated with pollution inputs were included in the sensitive and indifferent taxa (EGI), while those associated with pollution inputs were included in the tolerant and opportunistic taxa (EGIII)
steps.searchable_summary: Categorize EGs into EGI (sensitive/indifferent) and EGIII (tolerant/opportunistic) based on pollution association
outputs: Taxa categorized into EGI and EGIII

steps.description: To expand the initial taxa list, a literature review was done to identify microbial taxa frequently observed in environmental studies.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct literature review to expand the initial taxa list with frequently observed taxa

steps.description: The criteria for assigning these taxa to the two aforementioned EGs follow the approach outlined by Aylagas et al. [5]. Specifically, a taxon is assigned to EGIII when: (i) is dominant in organic matter-enriched sediments; (ii) exhibits response to organic pollution; (iii) is predominantly found in anoxic methane-rich sediments; (iv) is identified as a nitrite oxidizer and related to nitrogen
steps.searchable_summary: Assign expanded taxa to EGs (EGI or EGIII) based on defined criteria from Aylagas et al. [5]
outputs: Expanded taxa list with EG assignments

steps.description: Within each sample, the relative abundance ratio of EGI and EGIII was used to calculate the index
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the relative abundance ratio of EGI and EGIII within each sample
inputs: Taxa categorized into EGI and EGIII
outputs: Relative abundance ratio of EGI and EGIII per sample

steps.description: the relative abundance ratio of EGI and EGIII was used to calculate the index
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the microgAMBI index using the relative abundance ratio of EGI and EGIII
inputs: Relative abundance ratio of EGI and EGIII

steps.description: which provides an ecological classification of high, good, moderate, poor, or bad status [5]
steps.searchable_summary: Classify ecological status (high, good, moderate, poor, bad) based on the microgAMBI index value

methods.method_name: Taxa List Database Usage and Curation
methods.description: This method describes the structure and content of the taxa list database (GigaDB, Open Office template) used for microgAMBI calculation, including data preparation, using the template for calculation, accessing taxon information, and the process of database curation to ensure data quality.

steps.description: Access the taxa list database available in the GigaDB database [16], as an Open Office template allowing the calculation of microgAMBI.
steps.searchable_summary: Access the taxa list database template from GigaDB

steps.description: The first sheet is a stepwise guide... on how the user should prepare the data to calculate the index... (in the case of genus, without 'sp.'; hence, it is better to remove this in the datasets to be tested)
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare user data for microgAMBI calculation template, including removing 'sp.' from genus names
inputs: Raw user data (e.g., taxon abundances with genus names)

steps.description: The second one ('template'), is an empty spreadsheet template to be filled in with the data
steps.searchable_summary: Fill in the 'template' spreadsheet (Sheet 2) with the prepared user data

steps.description: ...and the necessary equations to obtain the ecological status by station, as originally described in Aylagas et al. [5].
steps.searchable_summary: Utilize the embedded equations in the 'template' spreadsheet to obtain the ecological status by station

steps.description: The third sheet is also available as a TSV file ('taxa list') that includes: (i) in column A, the correlative number of each taxon; (ii) in column B, the name of each taxon...; (iii) in column E, the assignation of taxa to a corresponding EG; (iv) in column G, the literature on which the decision to assign a taxon to an EG was based on; and (v) in column H, comments about the decision on assigning the taxon to an EG.
steps.searchable_summary: Refer to the 'taxa list' sheet (Sheet 3) for detailed information on each taxon
outputs: Taxon details (ID, name, EG, literature basis, comments)

steps.description: For each of them, the DOI (Digital Object Identifier) or other URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) links are provided in column F to access one or more papers supporting the assignation.
steps.searchable_summary: Access supporting literature for taxon assignments using provided DOI or URI links from the 'taxa list' sheet

steps.description: To ensure the quality of the data, the database was curated, checking the accepted taxa
steps.searchable_summary: Perform quality control and curation of the taxa list database by checking accepted taxa and verifying assignments/links
